expanded_description: A comprehensive examination of the sliding filament theory—the molecular mechanism explaining how muscles contract—detailing the interactions between thick (myosin) and thin (actin) filaments, the cross-bridge cycle, the roles of ATP and calcium in regulating contraction, the length-tension relationship, and the molecular basis of muscle force generation, relaxation, and energy metabolism.

core_concept.principle: The sliding filament theory states that muscle contraction occurs when thin (actin) filaments slide past thick (myosin) filaments, shortening the sarcomere without changing filament lengths, driven by cyclic cross-bridge formation between myosin heads and actin, powered by ATP hydrolysis, and regulated by calcium binding to troponin which exposes myosin-binding sites on actin.
core_concept.explanation: Proposed independently by Huxley and Niedergerke (1954) and Huxley and Hanson (1954), the sliding filament theory revolutionized understanding of muscle contraction. Key principles: (1) Sarcomere shortening during contraction occurs because thin filaments (actin) are pulled toward the M line by myosin heads; (2) Filament lengths remain constant—A band (thick filaments) does not change; I band (thin filaments only) shortens; H zone (thick filaments only) narrows or disappears; (3) Force generation results from cyclic attachment, power stroke, and detachment of myosin cross-bridges; (4) ATP provides energy for both the power stroke (through hydrolysis) and detachment (through binding); (5) Calcium acts as the regulatory switch—at low concentrations, tropomyosin blocks myosin-binding sites; at high concentrations, calcium binds troponin, causing tropomyosin movement and site exposure. The cross-bridge cycle comprises: (a) myosin head (with ADP+Pi) binds actin; (b) Pi release triggers power stroke (myosin pivots, pulling actin); (c) ADP release; (d) ATP binding causes myosin detachment; (e) ATP hydrolysis re-energizes myosin head to original position. Contraction continues as long as calcium remains elevated and ATP available. Relaxation occurs when calcium is pumped back into sarcoplasmic reticulum, tropomyosin returns to blocking position, and cross-bridges cease. The sliding filament theory explains the length-tension relationship (optimal overlap at resting length), force-velocity relationship (lighter loads move faster), and the molecular basis of muscle diseases (myosin mutations cause cardiomyopathy; troponin mutations affect calcium sensitivity).

image.alt: Sarcomere before and after contraction showing I band shortening, H zone disappearance, A band constant; actin filaments sliding past myosin
image.caption: Sliding filament theory: During contraction, thin (actin) filaments slide past thick (myosin) filaments. A band (thick filament length) constant; I band (thin only) shortens; H zone (thick only) disappears. Filament lengths unchanged.

sections.section_title: Introduction: The Molecular Basis of Muscle Contraction
sections.introduction: The sliding filament theory, proposed in 1954 by two independent research teams (Huxley & Niedergerke; Huxley & Hanson), provided the first molecular explanation for muscle contraction—resolving a century-old question of how muscles generate force. The theory elegantly explained that muscles do not shorten by folding or coiling of protein filaments, but by the sliding of thin (actin) filaments past thick (myosin) filaments, driven by energy from ATP and regulated by calcium. This mechanism operates at the sarcomere level—the repeating contractile unit of striated muscle. During contraction, myosin heads (projecting from thick filaments) bind to actin, undergo a conformational change (power stroke) that pulls actin toward the sarcomere center, then detach and repeat. Each cross-bridge cycle consumes one ATP molecule and produces a small amount of force (~2-3 pN); thousands of cycles occurring simultaneously across thousands of sarcomeres produce macroscopic force. The cycle is regulated by calcium: at rest, tropomyosin covers myosin-binding sites on actin; upon neural activation, calcium released from sarcoplasmic reticulum binds troponin, causing tropomyosin to move and expose binding sites. The sliding filament theory explains fundamental physiological phenomena: the length-tension relationship (optimal force at resting length where overlap maximizes cross-bridge formation), the force-velocity relationship (heavier loads move slower), and the dependence of contraction on ATP (rigor mortis after death). This section examines the molecular components, the cross-bridge cycle in detail, calcium regulation, energy requirements, and the structural basis of force generation—providing the mechanistic foundation for understanding muscle function in health and disease.

introduction_image.alt: Historical electron micrographs; Huxley and Hanson; sarcomere structure; cross-bridge visualization
introduction_image.caption: Sliding filament theory (Huxley & Hanson, 1954): Contraction occurs by actin sliding past myosin, not filament shortening. EM images show cross-bridges (myosin heads) extending to actin filaments.

key_concept.definition: The sliding filament theory explains muscle contraction as the sliding of thin (actin) filaments past thick (myosin) filaments, driven by ATP-dependent cross-bridge cycling, with calcium acting as the regulatory switch that exposes myosin-binding sites on actin.
key_concept.context: Key experimental evidence: (1) EM showed cross-bridges connecting thick and thin filaments; (2) A band length constant during contraction; I band and H zone shorten; (3) Myosin heads bind actin in presence of ATP; (4) ATPase activity of myosin correlates with contraction speed. Clinical relevance: Understanding sliding filament mechanism explains muscle weakness in myopathies (mutations affecting cross-bridge kinetics), cardiomyopathy (sarcomere mutations alter force generation), and rigor mortis (ATP depletion prevents detachment).

image.alt: Electron micrographs of contracted vs. relaxed sarcomere; A band constant; I band changes; cross-bridges
image.caption: Evidence for sliding filament: A band length constant (thick filaments); I band and H zone shorten (thin filaments slide inward). Cross-bridges (myosin heads) visible connecting thick and thin filaments.

sections.key_concept: Muscle contraction results from cyclic cross-bridge formation between myosin heads and actin, powered by ATP hydrolysis, regulated by calcium via troponin-tropomyosin, and dependent on sarcomere length for optimal force generation.

classifications.value: Best for understanding the ATP-driven cycle of attachment, force generation, and detachment
characteristics: Step 1: Resting (cocked) state—Myosin head (ADP + Pi bound) in high-energy configuration, not bound to actin
Step 2: Binding—Myosin head binds to actin (cross-bridge formation)
Step 3: Power stroke—Pi release triggers myosin head pivoting, pulling actin toward M line (force generated, ADP released)
Step 4: Detachment—ATP binding causes myosin head to detach from actin
Step 5: Re-energizing—ATP hydrolysis re-cocks myosin head to high-energy state (ADP + Pi bound), ready for next cycle
classifications.behavior: The cross-bridge cycle repeats 5-10 times per second during sustained contraction (faster in fast-twitch fibers). Each cycle produces ~2-3 pN force and moves actin ~10 nm. Power stroke: myosin head rotates ~70° (lever arm movement), driven by conformational changes in myosin head upon Pi release. The myosin lever arm (light chain domain) amplifies small conformational changes into larger movement. ATP binding not only provides energy but also reduces myosin-actin affinity, enabling detachment. ATP hydrolysis (myosin ATPase) occurs after detachment, re-energizing the head. The cycle continues as long as: (1) calcium elevated (sites exposed), (2) ATP available, (3) myosin heads able to bind. Contraction stops when calcium falls (pumped into SR), allowing tropomyosin to block binding sites. In rigor mortis, ATP depletion after death prevents detachment, causing permanent cross-bridge formation and muscle rigidity.

examples.explanation: Five-step cross-bridge cycle: (1) Cocked state: Myosin head (with ADP and Pi bound) in high-energy conformation (lever arm up). ATP hydrolysis already occurred. (2) Binding: Calcium-exposed actin site allows myosin head to bind (weak binding, then strong binding). (3) Power stroke: Pi release triggers conformational change—myosin head pivots (lever arm swings ~70°), pulling actin filament ~10 nm. ADP released. (4) Detachment: ATP binds to myosin head, reducing actin affinity, causing detachment. (5) Re-energizing: Myosin ATPase hydrolyzes ATP → ADP + Pi, re-cocking myosin head (lever arm returns to up position). Significance: Each cycle consumes 1 ATP, moves actin 10 nm, produces 2-3 pN force. In whole muscle, millions of cycles sum to produce macroscopic force. Myosin ATPase rate determines contraction speed (fast fibers have higher ATPase).

image.alt: Cross-bridge cycle five steps: cocked, binding, power stroke, detachment, re-energizing; ATP roles
image.caption: Cross-bridge cycle: 1) Cocked (ADP+Pi); 2) Actin binding; 3) Pi release → power stroke (force); 4) ATP binding → detachment; 5) ATP hydrolysis → re-cocking. 1 ATP per cycle.

examples.process: Rigor Mortis: ATP Depletion and Permanent Cross-Bridges
examples.explanation: Rigor mortis mechanism: (1) After death, ATP production ceases; (2) Existing ATP depleted (hours); (3) Without ATP, myosin heads remain bound to actin (cannot detach—ATP required for detachment); (4) Cross-bridges locked in rigor state (myosin heads in power stroke position, not re-cocked); (5) Muscle becomes rigid (stiff). Onset: 2-6 hours post-mortem; full rigor 12-24 hours; resolves 24-72 hours as proteolysis breaks down proteins. Significance: Demonstrates absolute requirement for ATP in detachment step; explains why ATP is essential for relaxation. Clinical: Rigor mortis used to estimate time of death; also occurs in living muscle during ischemic contracture (no blood flow, ATP depletion).

image.alt: Rigor mortis: ATP depletion → cross-bridges locked; time course; ischemic contracture
image.caption: Rigor mortis: ATP depletion prevents myosin detachment → cross-bridges locked in rigor state → muscle rigidity. Onset 2-6 hours; full 12-24 hours; resolves with proteolysis. Demonstrates ATP requirement for detachment.

classifications.value: Best for understanding excitation-contraction coupling and the calcium switch mechanism
characteristics: Tropomyosin: Coiled-coil dimer lying in groove of actin helix; at rest, covers myosin-binding sites on actin
Troponin complex: TnT (binds tropomyosin), TnI (inhibitory—binds actin, blocks myosin binding), TnC (calcium-binding—EF-hand motifs)
Low [Ca²⁺] (<0.1 μM): TnC no Ca²⁺; TnI bound to actin; tropomyosin in blocking position
High [Ca²⁺] (1-10 μM): Ca²⁺ binds TnC (4 sites in skeletal, 1 site in cardiac); conformational change → TnI releases actin; tropomyosin moves deeper into actin groove, exposing myosin-binding sites
Relaxation: Ca²⁺ pumped into SR (SERCA); TnC loses Ca²⁺; tropomyosin returns to blocking position
classifications.behavior: Calcium acts as the on/off switch for striated muscle contraction. At rest, sarcoplasmic [Ca²⁺] ~0.1 μM; tropomyosin blocks myosin binding sites. Upon excitation, Ca²⁺ released from SR (via ryanodine receptors) raises [Ca²⁺] to 1-10 μM. Ca²⁺ binds TnC (EF-hand domains) causing conformational change transmitted through TnI, TnT to tropomyosin, moving it ~10-15 Å into actin groove. This exposes 7-14 myosin-binding sites per tropomyosin molecule, allowing cross-bridge cycling. Contraction strength (force) depends on [Ca²⁺] (graded response). Relaxation: Ca²⁺ pumped back into SR by SERCA (sarcoplasmic/endoplasmic reticulum calcium ATPase); [Ca²⁺] falls; tropomyosin returns to blocking position; cross-bridges cease. Cardiac vs. skeletal differences: Cardiac TnC has only 1 Ca²⁺-binding site (skeletal has 4); cardiac troponin mutations cause cardiomyopathy; cardiac relaxation requires Ca²⁺ removal from TnC, which is slower (diastolic dysfunction in heart failure).

examples.process: TnC Calcium Binding and Tropomyosin Movement
examples.explanation: TnC structure and function: (1) EF-hand calcium-binding domains (N-terminal regulatory sites, C-terminal structural sites); (2) Skeletal TnC: 4 Ca²⁺-binding sites (2 regulatory, 2 structural); (3) Cardiac TnC: Only 1 regulatory Ca²⁺-binding site; (4) Ca²⁺ binding induces conformational change: TnC hydrophobic patch exposed → binds TnI; (5) TnI releases actin; TnT transmits movement to tropomyosin; (6) Tropomyosin moves ~10-15 Å, exposing myosin-binding sites. Significance: TnC is the calcium sensor; cardiac TnC lower Ca²⁺ sensitivity explains less steep force-[Ca²⁺] relationship; TnC mutations cause cardiomyopathy (hypertrophic, dilated). Clinical: Calcium sensitizers (levosimendan) increase TnC Ca²⁺ sensitivity, used in heart failure; troponin I and T are cardiac-specific biomarkers for myocardial infarction.

image.alt: TnC EF-hand domains; Ca²⁺ binding; conformational change; tropomyosin movement; cardiac vs. skeletal
image.caption: TnC: EF-hand Ca²⁺-binding domains. Ca²⁺ binding → TnC conformation change → TnI releases actin → tropomyosin moves → myosin-binding sites exposed. Cardiac TnC has 1 regulatory site (lower Ca²⁺ sensitivity).

examples.explanation: Relaxation requires Ca²⁺ removal: (1) SERCA (sarcoplasmic reticulum Ca²⁺-ATPase) pumps Ca²⁺ from cytosol into SR; (2) SERCA transports 2 Ca²⁺ per ATP; (3) Phospholamban (PLB) regulates SERCA in cardiac muscle (dephosphorylated PLB inhibits SERCA; PKA phosphorylation relieves inhibition); (4) Ca²⁺ also extruded via NCX (Na⁺/Ca²⁺ exchanger) in cardiac muscle; (5) Relaxation rate depends on SERCA activity. Clinical: Heart failure → decreased SERCA activity, decreased PLB phosphorylation → slower relaxation (diastolic dysfunction); β-agonists increase PLB phosphorylation (PKA) → faster relaxation. Significance: Calcium removal is energy-dependent (ATP for SERCA) and rate-limiting for relaxation; impaired relaxation causes diastolic heart failure.

image.alt: SERCA pump; phospholamban regulation; NCX; relaxation; heart failure
image.caption: Relaxation: SERCA pumps Ca²⁺ into SR (2 Ca²⁺/ATP). Phospholamban (PLB) inhibits SERCA; PKA phosphorylation relieves inhibition. Heart failure → decreased SERCA → slower relaxation (diastolic dysfunction).

classifications.type: ATP in Muscle Contraction: Energy Requirements
classifications.value: Best for understanding energy sources, ATP consumption, and metabolic support for contraction
characteristics: ATP uses: (1) Myosin ATPase—cross-bridge cycling (power stroke, detachment); (2) SERCA—calcium pumping into SR (relaxation); (3) Na⁺/K⁺ ATPase—restoring ionic gradients

ATP sources: (1) Creatine phosphate (immediate—1 ATP per creatine phosphate); (2) Glycolysis (short-term—2 ATP/glucose anaerobic; 38 ATP/glucose aerobic); (3) Oxidative phosphorylation (long-term—34-36 ATP/glucose)
ATP consumption rate: Resting muscle ~0.1 μmol/g/min; intense exercise ~100-200 μmol/g/min
Creatine kinase: Regenerates ATP from ADP + creatine phosphate; provides ~5-10 seconds of high-intensity contraction
Myosin ATPase isoforms: Type I (slow) low ATPase; Type IIb (fast) high ATPase
classifications.behavior: ATP is consumed at multiple steps during contraction-relaxation cycle: myosin ATPase (cross-bridge cycling) consumes most ATP during contraction; SERCA consumes ATP during relaxation; Na⁺/K⁺ ATPase consumes ATP to maintain resting membrane potential. Muscle stores minimal ATP (~5 mmol/kg wet weight)—enough for 1-2 seconds of maximal contraction. Creatine phosphate (CP) provides immediate ATP regeneration: creatine kinase (CK) transfers phosphate from CP to ADP, producing ATP. CP stores (~20-30 mmol/kg) provide ~5-10 seconds of high-intensity activity. For longer activity, glycogenolysis and oxidative phosphorylation supply ATP. Fast-twitch fibers have high myosin ATPase activity, contract rapidly but fatigue quickly; slow-twitch fibers have low ATPase, slower contraction but fatigue-resistant. In ischemia (e.g., myocardial infarction), ATP depletion → impaired relaxation (diastolic contracture), then impaired contraction, then cell death. Clinical: Creatine kinase elevated in muscle injury (CK-MM), myocardial infarction (CK-MB); mitochondrial myopathies impair oxidative ATP production → exercise intolerance, fatigue.

examples.process: Creatine Phosphate System: Immediate Energy Reserve
examples.explanation: Creatine phosphate (CP) system: (1) Creatine kinase (CK) catalyzes: CP + ADP ↔ creatine + ATP; (2) CP stores 4-6× ATP concentration; (3) Provides ATP for ~5-10 seconds of maximal contraction; (4) Replenished during rest (aerobic recovery); (5) Distribution: highest in fast-twitch fibers (sprinters have high CK activity). Significance: CP system provides immediate energy for explosive movements (sprinting, jumping). Athletic training increases CP stores. Clinical: CK elevation in muscle injury (rhabdomyolysis, muscular dystrophy); CK-MB elevated in myocardial infarction; creatine supplementation increases CP stores, enhances high-intensity performance.

image.alt: Creatine kinase reaction; CP stores; ATP regeneration; CK as biomarker; creatine supplementation
image.caption: Creatine phosphate: CP + ADP ←CK→ creatine + ATP. Provides immediate ATP regeneration (5-10 seconds). CK elevated in muscle injury (CK-MM), MI (CK-MB). Creatine supplementation increases CP stores.

examples.process: Myosin ATPase Isoforms and Contraction Speed
examples.explanation: Myosin heavy chain isoforms: (1) MHC I (slow, β-cardiac): Low ATPase activity; slow contraction; fatigue-resistant; (2) MHC IIa (fast oxidative): Intermediate ATPase; moderate contraction speed; fatigue moderately; (3) MHC IIb/x (fast glycolytic): High ATPase activity; rapid contraction; fatigable; (4) ATPase activity correlates with contraction velocity (unloaded shortening velocity); (5) Fiber typing: Myosin ATPase histochemistry (acid/alkaline pre-incubation) distinguishes fiber types. Significance: Myosin ATPase determines contraction speed; fast fibers (IIb) have 3-5× higher ATPase than slow fibers (I). Training: endurance training shifts IIb → IIa; resistance training increases fiber size, minimal type conversion. Clinical: Myosin heavy chain mutations cause myopathies (slow myosin mutations → congenital myopathy; fast myosin mutations → distal myopathy).

image.alt: MHC isoforms; ATPase activity; contraction speed; fiber typing; training adaptations
image.caption: Myosin ATPase isoforms: MHC I (slow, low ATPase) → slow contraction; MHC IIa (intermediate); MHC IIb (fast, high ATPase) → rapid contraction. ATPase activity determines contraction velocity.

sections.section_title: Fundamental Principles of Sliding Filament Theory

principles.principle: Length-Tension Relationship: Sarcomere Length Determines Force
principles.statement: The force generated by a muscle fiber depends on sarcomere length—maximal at resting length (2.0-2.2 μm) where actin-myosin overlap maximizes cross-bridge formation; force decreases at shorter lengths (filament collision, reduced overlap) and longer lengths (reduced overlap).
principles.application: Physiological inference: (1) Resting length = optimal overlap = maximal active force; (2) Shortened muscle (e.g., flexed joint) produces less force; (3) Stretched muscle (e.g., extended joint) produces less active force (but more passive force from titin). Clinical application: Rehabilitation—exercising at optimal length maximizes strength gains; contracture management—stretching maintains sarcomere length, prevents shortening adaptation. Evolutionary insight: Length-tension relationship is universal in striated muscle; sarcomere length optimized for functional demands (cardiac operates on ascending limb, sensitive to filling).
principles.example: Length-tension relationship in human muscle: (1) Biceps curl: At full extension (long length) → reduced active force (less overlap); at mid-range (resting length) → maximal force; at full flexion (short length) → reduced force (filament collision); (2) Clinical: Immobilization in shortened position (casting) → sarcomeres lost in series, muscle shortens permanently (contracture); (3) Stretching: Passive stretch increases sarcomere length, activates titin-based passive tension, stimulates protein synthesis; (4) Cardiac: Frank-Starling relationship—increased preload stretches sarcomeres, increasing active force (up to optimal overlap). Significance: Length-tension relationship explains muscle function across joint angles; guides rehabilitation and surgical tendon transfers.
principles.analogy: Length-tension is like a rope pull: If rope is too short (overlapped), pullers interfere; if rope is too long (stretched), fewer pullers can reach. Optimal length lets all pullers (myosin heads) grab the rope (actin) and pull effectively.

image.alt: Length-tension curve: active, passive, total tension; sarcomere overlap at different lengths
image.caption: Length-tension relationship: Maximal active force at resting length (2.0-2.2 μm) where cross-bridge overlap optimal. Shorter lengths (filament collision) and longer lengths (reduced overlap) decrease force. Passive tension from titin at long lengths.

principles.principle: Force-Velocity Relationship: Load Affects Contraction Speed
principles.statement: The velocity of muscle shortening is inversely related to the load—lighter loads shorten faster; heavier loads shorten slower; at isometric loads (load > maximal force), no shortening occurs; this relationship reflects cross-bridge cycling kinetics.
principles.application: Physiological inference: (1) Zero load: maximal shortening velocity (Vmax); (2) Increasing load reduces velocity; (3) Isometric: load equals force, no shortening; (4) Eccentric contraction: load exceeds force, muscle lengthens (lower energy cost, greater force). Clinical application: Rehabilitation—eccentric training effective for tendinopathy, muscle hypertrophy; force-velocity explains movement speed limitations in strength training. Evolutionary insight: Fast-twitch fibers have higher Vmax (high myosin ATPase); slow-twitch fibers lower Vmax; fiber type distribution matches functional demands (sprinters >90% fast fibers; marathoners >80% slow fibers).
principles.example: Force-velocity in practice: (1) Lifting light weight (10% 1RM) → high velocity; lifting heavy weight (90% 1RM) → low velocity; (2) Eccentric contraction (lowering weight) → higher force at same velocity; (3) Sprinting: Fast-twitch fibers (high Vmax) recruited for high-velocity movements; (4) Clinical: Velocity-dependent weakness in myopathies (reduced Vmax); spasticity (velocity-dependent increased resistance). Significance: Force-velocity relationship explains why maximal strength training (heavy loads) requires slow movement; power training (light loads, high velocity) improves explosive performance.
principles.analogy: Force-velocity is like bicycle gears: Heavy gear (high load) = slow pedaling (low velocity); light gear (low load) = fast pedaling (high velocity). Engine (muscle) has optimal power at intermediate load and velocity.

image.alt: Force-velocity curve: concentric, isometric, eccentric; Vmax; fiber type differences
image.caption: Force-velocity: Inverse relationship—lighter loads shorten faster; heavier loads slower. Eccentric contractions produce greater force at same velocity. Fast fibers have higher Vmax (steeper curve).

principles.principle: Calcium Regulates Contraction Strength via Graded Activation
principles.statement: The strength (force) of contraction is graded by intracellular calcium concentration—higher [Ca²⁺] recruits more cross-bridges (more binding sites exposed), generating greater force; this enables graded, smooth contractions rather than all-or-none fiber responses.
principles.application: Physiological inference: (1) Twitch: single action potential → transient Ca²⁺ release → brief contraction; (2) Summation: multiple APs increase [Ca²⁺] → greater force; (3) Tetanus: sustained high [Ca²⁺] → maximal force; (4) Motor unit recruitment: small units first (type I), larger units as force needed. Clinical application: Heart failure (impaired Ca²⁺ handling → reduced contractility); malignant hyperthermia (uncontrolled Ca²⁺ release → sustained contraction, hypermetabolism); calcium sensitizers (levosimendan) increase force without increasing Ca²⁺. Evolutionary insight: Calcium regulation allows precise force control; cardiac muscle has steeper force-[Ca²⁺] relationship than skeletal (tuned for Frank-Starling mechanism).
principles.example: Graded force in skeletal muscle: (1) Single twitch (low force); (2) Summation (moderate force); (3) Tetanus (maximal force); (4) Motor unit recruitment: small motor units (type I) for fine control (extraocular muscles); large units for power (gastrocnemius). Clinical: Myasthenia gravis (reduced ACh receptors) → fewer fibers activated → weakness; botulinum toxin (blocks ACh release) → reduced activation, used for spasticity, dystonia. Significance: Calcium and recruitment mechanisms enable smooth, controlled force production from fine movements (finger) to powerful contractions (quadriceps).
principles.analogy: Calcium grading is like dimmer switch: Low calcium = dim light (low force); more calcium = brighter light (more force). Motor unit recruitment is like adding light bulbs—first small bulbs (type I), then larger bulbs (type II) as more light (force) needed.

image.alt: Twitch, summation, tetanus; force-[Ca²⁺] relationship; motor unit recruitment; clinical correlations
image.caption: Graded force: [Ca²⁺] determines cross-bridge recruitment. Single twitch (low Ca²⁺, low force); summation (higher Ca²⁺); tetanus (maximal Ca²⁺, maximal force). Motor unit recruitment adds fibers for graded force.

sections.section_title: Key Components of Sliding Filament Mechanism

components.definition: The sliding filament mechanism integrates myosin cross-bridges (force generators), actin filaments (track), ATP (energy source), calcium-troponin-tropomyosin (regulatory switch), and sarcomere geometry (length-dependence).
components.equation: $\text{Force} \propto \text{[Ca}^{2+}\text{]} \times \text{Cross-bridge cycling rate} \times \text{Number of cross-bridges} \times \text{Sarcomere overlap factor}$
components.interpretation: Muscle force depends on calcium concentration (determines number of available binding sites), cross-bridge cycling rate (myosin ATPase isoform), number of cross-bridges (fiber cross-sectional area), and sarcomere overlap (length-tension relationship).

image.alt: Integrated diagram: cross-bridge cycle, calcium regulation, length-tension, force-velocity
image.caption: Sliding filament integration: Cross-bridge cycle (ATP-powered) + Calcium regulation (troponin-tropomyosin) + Length-tension (sarcomere overlap) + Force-velocity (load dependence) = controlled muscle contraction.

categories.type: Myosin Lever Arm and Power Stroke Amplification
categories.description: The myosin head contains a lever arm (light chain binding domain) that amplifies small conformational changes into large movement (~10 nm per power stroke); lever arm length varies among myosin isoforms, affecting contraction speed and force.
categories.detailed_mechanism: Lever arm mechanism: (1) Myosin head consists of motor domain (actin binding, ATPase) and lever arm (light chain binding domain); (2) Upon Pi release, motor domain undergoes small conformational change (~5° rotation); (3) Lever arm amplifies this rotation (lever arm length ~8-12 nm) into ~70° swing, moving actin ~10 nm; (4) Longer lever arm = greater displacement per ATP (more efficient) but slower cycling (inertia); shorter lever arm = faster cycling but smaller displacement. Isoforms: Fast myosin has shorter lever arm (higher velocity); slow myosin has longer lever arm (more efficient). Significance: Lever arm geometry determines contraction mechanics; mutations in lever arm region cause myopathies, cardiomyopathy.

examples.process: Myosin Lever Arm Mutations and Myopathy
examples.explanation: Myosin light chain (lever arm) mutations: (1) Essential light chain (ELC) and regulatory light chain (RLC) mutations; (2) Effects: altered lever arm stiffness, reduced power stroke amplitude, impaired force generation; (3) Clinical: Myosin storage myopathy (slow myosin mutations), distal myopathy (fast myosin), hypertrophic cardiomyopathy (β-myosin light chain mutations); (4) Mechanism: Mutations disrupt lever arm function, reducing force and/or altering calcium sensitivity. Significance: Lever arm geometry critical for force generation; mutations cause inherited myopathies, cardiomyopathies. Understanding lever arm mechanics informs therapeutic design (myosin modulators).

image.alt: Myosin lever arm; light chains; power stroke amplification; mutations causing myopathy
image.caption: Myosin lever arm (light chain binding domain) amplifies motor domain rotation into 10 nm movement. Light chain mutations disrupt lever arm function → reduced force, myopathy, cardiomyopathy.

categories.description: Actin filament is a double helix of G-actin monomers; each G-actin has a myosin-binding site that is blocked by tropomyosin at rest; activation moves tropomyosin, exposing 7-14 sites per tropomyosin molecule.
categories.detailed_mechanism: Actin structure: (1) G-actin (globular) monomers polymerize into F-actin (filamentous) double helix; (2) Each G-actin has myosin-binding site; (3) Tropomyosin lies in actin groove, covering 7-14 binding sites; (4) One troponin complex per tropomyosin; (5) Activation: Ca²⁺ binds TnC → tropomyosin moves into groove → binding sites exposed. Cooperativity: Tropomyosin movement exposes multiple sites (cooperative activation); binding of one myosin head can further expose adjacent sites (positive feedback). Significance: Cooperativity enables steep force-[Ca²⁺] relationship; mutations in actin cause nemaline myopathy, cardiomyopathy.

examples.explanation: ACTA1 (skeletal α-actin) mutations: (1) Dominant or recessive mutations; (2) Effects: impaired actin polymerization, altered myosin binding, disrupted thin filament stability; (3) Pathology: nemaline rods (α-actinin, actin aggregates) in muscle fibers; (4) Clinical: Severe neonatal hypotonia, respiratory failure (lethal) to milder childhood-onset; (5) Genotype-phenotype correlation: Most dominant mutations severe; some missense milder. Significance: Actin is essential for force generation; mutations disrupt thin filament function, causing congenital myopathy. Therapy: None curative; supportive (respiratory, nutritional).

image.alt: ACTA1 mutations; nemaline rods; clinical spectrum; actin structure
image.caption: ACTA1 (α-actin) mutations cause nemaline myopathy. Pathology: nemaline rods (α-actinin, actin aggregates). Severe neonatal (hypotonia, respiratory failure) to mild adult-onset forms.

sections.section_title: Worked Example: Applying Sliding Filament Theory

worked_examples.title: Analyzing Muscle Contraction in Different Conditions
worked_examples.problem: Compare three scenarios: (A) Maximal voluntary contraction at optimal muscle length; (B) Muscle stretched to 1.5× resting length; (C) Muscle in rigor mortis. Using sliding filament theory principles: (1) How does force generation differ? (2) What cross-bridge and regulatory states explain each? (3) What is the ATP requirement? (4) How do these scenarios illustrate sliding filament principles?

step_by_step.explanation: Analyze force generation in each scenario.
step_by_step.calculation: (A) Optimal length: maximal active force (optimal cross-bridge overlap; maximal number of cross-bridges). (B) Stretched: reduced active force (less overlap, fewer cross-bridges); increased passive force (titin elasticity). (C) Rigor: passive force + rigor cross-bridges (myosin heads bound but not cycling); no active force generation.

(A) Active contraction: Ca²⁺ elevated → tropomyosin moved → cross-bridge cycling (ATP-dependent); (B) Stretched: If not stimulated, resting state (Ca²⁺ low, tropomyosin blocking); if stimulated, active force reduced (less overlap); (C) Rigor: ATP depleted → myosin heads bound to actin in power stroke position (cannot detach); tropomyosin may be in blocking position but myosin heads still bound (rigor complex).

(A) High ATP consumption: myosin ATPase (cross-bridge cycling) + SERCA (Ca²⁺ pumping) + Na⁺/K⁺ ATPase; (B) Resting: low ATP (basal metabolism); stimulated: ATP consumption reduced vs. optimal length (fewer cross-bridges); (C) No ATP consumption (none available); rigor state persists.

(A) Demonstrates: length-tension (optimal overlap), Ca²⁺ regulation (excitation-contraction coupling), cross-bridge cycling (ATP-dependent); (B) Demonstrates: length-tension (reduced overlap reduces force), passive tension (titin); (C) Demonstrates: ATP requirement for detachment (rigor without ATP), cross-bridge cycle (trapped in power stroke state).

worked_examples.final_answer: (A) Optimal length: maximal active force (optimal overlap), high ATP consumption (cycling), Ca²⁺-regulated cycling. (B) Stretched: reduced active force (less overlap), increased passive tension (titin), lower ATP consumption if stimulated. (C) Rigor: no active force (ATP depletion), cross-bridges locked (cannot detach), no ATP consumption. Illustrates: length-tension, ATP requirement, Ca²⁺ regulation, passive elasticity.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Sliding filament theory explains diverse contraction states: active force depends on sarcomere length (overlap) and Ca²⁺ (binding site exposure); ATP is essential for cross-bridge cycling and relaxation; rigor demonstrates ATP requirement for detachment. These principles apply to normal physiology, exercise, and post-mortem changes.

sections.section_title: Process Flow: From Excitation to Contraction
sections.introduction: Muscle contraction proceeds from neural activation to force generation through excitation-contraction coupling, cross-bridge cycling, and relaxation. Understanding this flow integrates sliding filament theory with physiological control.

process_flow.description: Motor neuron releases ACh, depolarizing sarcolemma and initiating action potential.
process_flow.mechanism: ACh binds nicotinic receptors → Na⁺ influx → end-plate potential → voltage-gated Na⁺ channels open → action potential propagates along sarcolemma and down T-tubules.
process_flow.result: Membrane depolarization; T-tubule depolarization activates DHPR

image.alt: NMJ; ACh release; action potential; T-tubule depolarization
image.caption: Step 1: Excitation—Motor neuron ACh → end-plate potential → action potential → T-tubule depolarization. Initiates excitation-contraction coupling.

process_flow.description: T-tubule depolarization triggers Ca²⁺ release from sarcoplasmic reticulum.
process_flow.mechanism: DHPR (L-type Ca²⁺ channel) in T-tubule detects depolarization → activates RyR1 (ryanodine receptor) in SR → Ca²⁺ released from SR into cytosol (skeletal: mechanical coupling; cardiac: calcium-induced).
process_flow.result: Cytosolic [Ca²⁺] rises from 0.1 μM to 1-10 μM

image.alt: DHPR-RyR coupling; SR Ca²⁺ release; Ca²⁺ sparks
image.caption: Step 2: Calcium release—T-tubule depolarization activates DHPR → RyR1 opens → Ca²⁺ released from SR → cytosolic [Ca²⁺] rises (1-10 μM).

process_flow.description: Ca²⁺ binds troponin, exposing myosin-binding sites; cross-bridge cycling begins.
process_flow.mechanism: Ca²⁺ binds TnC → tropomyosin moves into actin groove → myosin-binding sites exposed → myosin heads bind actin → cross-bridge cycle (ATP-dependent) → force generation.
process_flow.result: Cross-bridge cycling; actin pulled toward M line; sarcomere shortens

image.alt: Ca²⁺ binds TnC; tropomyosin movement; cross-bridge binding; power stroke
image.caption: Step 3: Cross-bridge activation—Ca²⁺ binds TnC → tropomyosin moves → myosin heads bind actin → power stroke (actin slides) → sarcomere shortens.

process_flow.description: Ca²⁺ pumped back into SR; tropomyosin returns to blocking position; cross-bridges cease.
process_flow.mechanism: Ca²⁺ pumped into SR by SERCA (sarcoplasmic/endoplasmic reticulum Ca²⁺-ATPase); [Ca²⁺] falls below 0.1 μM → Ca²⁺ dissociates from TnC → tropomyosin returns to blocking position → no new cross-bridges form; existing cross-bridges detach (ATP-dependent).
process_flow.result: Muscle relaxes; returns to resting length (passive forces)

image.alt: SERCA pump; Ca²⁺ sequestration; tropomyosin returns; cross-bridge detachment
image.caption: Step 4: Relaxation—SERCA pumps Ca²⁺ into SR → [Ca²⁺] falls → Ca²⁺ leaves TnC → tropomyosin blocks sites → cross-bridges detach (ATP-dependent) → muscle relaxes.

sections.section_title: Advanced Concepts: Molecular Motors and Mechanics
sections.definition: Myosin is a molecular motor that converts chemical energy (ATP hydrolysis) into mechanical work (force, movement) through a conserved mechanism shared with other motor proteins (kinesin, dynein).

process_types.type: Myosin Motor Domain: Structure and Mechanism
process_types.energy_profile: Myosin head contains nucleotide-binding pocket (ATP), actin-binding interface, and converter domain (attached to lever arm); ATP hydrolysis and product release drive conformational changes
process_types.direction: ATP binding → myosin detaches from actin → ATP hydrolysis (re-energizing) → myosin cocks → weak actin binding → Pi release → strong binding → power stroke → ADP release
process_types.function: Convert ATP chemical energy into mechanical work (force, movement)
goals: Explain ATPase cycle and force generation
process_types.energy_source: X-ray crystallography, cryo-EM, single-molecule biophysics, molecular dynamics

primary_example.process: Myosin Motor Domain Mutations in Cardiomyopathy
primary_example.explanation: β-myosin heavy chain motor domain mutations: (1) R403Q (arginine 403 to glutamine) in actin-binding interface; (2) Effect: increased ATPase activity, increased force generation, altered cross-bridge kinetics; (3) Pathology: hypertrophic cardiomyopathy (asymmetric septal hypertrophy, myocyte disarray, fibrosis); (4) Mechanism: hypercontractility → increased energy demand → impaired relaxation → hypertrophy; (5) Therapeutic: Myosin inhibitors (mavacamten) reduce cross-bridge formation, normalize contractility. Significance: Motor domain mutations demonstrate how altered molecular mechanics cause disease; understanding motor function enables targeted therapy.

image.alt: Myosin motor domain structure; ATP pocket; actin-binding; R403Q mutation; mavacamten
image.caption: Myosin motor domain: ATP-binding pocket + actin-binding interface. R403Q mutation (HCM) increases ATPase, hypercontractility. Mavacamten (myosin inhibitor) reduces cross-bridge formation, normalizes contraction.

image.alt: Myosin motor cycle with ATP hydrolysis, conformational changes; structural states
image.caption: Myosin motor cycle: ATP binding (detachment) → hydrolysis (re-energizing) → Pi release (power stroke) → ADP release (rigor). Structural states determined by nucleotide binding.

process_types.energy_profile: Cross-bridge attachment/detachment rates determine contraction velocity and force; myosin ATPase rate sets cycling speed
process_types.direction: Attachment rate (f) depends on [Ca²⁺] and overlap; detachment rate (g) depends on ATP availability and myosin isoform; duty ratio = time attached / cycle time
process_types.function: Determine contraction speed, force, and energy efficiency
process_types.energy_source: Skinned fiber mechanics, laser trap assays, kinetic modeling

primary_example.explanation: Myosin heavy chain isoforms determine kinetics: (1) Type I (slow): low ATPase (0.3-0.5 s⁻¹), slow attachment/detachment rates, high duty ratio (long attachment time), low velocity, high economy; (2) Type IIb (fast): high ATPase (3-5 s⁻¹), fast rates, low duty ratio, high velocity, low economy; (3) Force-velocity: Fast fibers have steeper force-velocity curve (higher Vmax); (4) ATP consumption: Fast fibers consume 3-5× more ATP per second. Clinical: Fiber type transitions in disease, aging; selective vulnerability in some myopathies (type II atrophy in disuse, glucocorticoid excess). Significance: Cross-bridge kinetics determine muscle performance; fiber type composition matches functional demands (posture vs. power).

image.alt: Attachment/detachment rates; duty ratio; force-velocity; myosin ATPase isoforms
image.caption: Cross-bridge kinetics: Fast myosin (IIb): high ATPase, fast rates, low duty ratio → high Vmax, low economy. Slow myosin (I): low ATPase, slow rates, high duty ratio → low Vmax, high economy (efficient).

image.alt: Kinetic model of cross-bridge cycle; rate constants; force-velocity derivation
image.caption: Cross-bridge kinetics: Attachment rate (f), detachment rate (g). Force-velocity relationship derived from f and g; Vmax proportional to detachment rate; economy inversely proportional to ATPase.

sections.relationship: Myosin is a molecular motor that powers muscle contraction; its motor domain structure and kinetics determine contraction velocity, force, and energy efficiency. Mutations in motor domain cause cardiomyopathy, myopathies; understanding molecular mechanisms enables targeted therapies (myosin inhibitors).

sections.section_title: Molecular Mechanisms: Force Generation and Regulation
sections.purpose: Force generation at the molecular level involves actin-myosin interaction, lever arm movement, and strain-dependent kinetics; regulation occurs via calcium-troponin and phosphorylation of myosin light chains.

activation.definition: Myosin heads undergo a power stroke when Pi is released from the active site; the lever arm amplifies motor domain rotation; force depends on number of cross-bridges and their strain state. Regulatory light chain (RLC) phosphorylation increases myosin ATPase activity (smooth muscle, cardiac).
activation.effect: The power stroke produces ~2-3 pN force and 10 nm displacement per cross-bridge. Total force = sum of cross-bridge forces × number of active cross-bridges. Strain-dependent kinetics: attached cross-bridges detach faster under negative strain (lengthening). RLC phosphorylation (by MLCK) increases myosin ATPase, enhances force at submaximal Ca²⁺ (smooth muscle). Cardiac RLC phosphorylation increases contractility (via PKA, PKC).

image.alt: Power stroke; lever arm movement; strain-dependent kinetics; RLC phosphorylation
image.caption: Molecular force generation: Pi release triggers lever arm swing (10 nm, 2-3 pN). Force = Σ cross-bridge forces × number active. RLC phosphorylation modulates ATPase (smooth, cardiac).

key_properties.explanation: Cross-bridge detachment rate depends on strain (force): (1) Positive strain (myosin pulling against load): normal detachment rate; (2) Negative strain (myosin being pulled backward, eccentric contraction): detachment rate increases (protective mechanism); (3) Effect: during eccentric contractions, fewer cross-bridges attached at any time, but each cross-bridge generates higher force; (4) Clinical: Eccentric contractions produce greater force with lower metabolic cost, but cause more muscle damage (DOMS). Significance: Strain-dependent kinetics explains force enhancement during eccentric contractions; protects muscle from overstretch injury; underlies adaptation to eccentric training.

image.alt: Strain-dependent detachment; eccentric vs. concentric; DOMS; training adaptations
image.caption: Strain-dependent kinetics: Negative strain (eccentric) increases detachment rate, but each cross-bridge generates higher force. Explains greater force, lower metabolic cost, and DOMS after eccentric exercise.

key_properties.explanation: RLC phosphorylation (by MLCK, myosin light chain kinase): (1) Increases myosin ATPase activity (2-3×); (2) Increases Ca²⁺ sensitivity (force at given [Ca²⁺]); (3) In smooth muscle: required for contraction (phosphorylation permissive); (4) In cardiac muscle: increases contractility (β-adrenergic stimulation → PKA → RLC phosphorylation); (5) In skeletal muscle: potentiates twitch (post-tetanic potentiation). Significance: RLC phosphorylation modulates contraction strength independently of Ca²⁺; underlies 'warm-up' potentiation in skeletal muscle; contributes to inotropic reserve in cardiac muscle. Clinical: Heart failure may involve impaired RLC phosphorylation; MLCK targets for therapeutic modulation.

image.alt: RLC phosphorylation by MLCK; effects on ATPase, Ca²⁺ sensitivity; post-tetanic potentiation
image.caption: Regulatory light chain (RLC) phosphorylation: MLCK phosphorylates RLC → increases myosin ATPase, Ca²⁺ sensitivity. Skeletal: post-tetanic potentiation; cardiac: increased contractility (β-adrenergic); smooth: required for contraction.

summary: The sliding filament theory (Huxley & Hanson, 1954) explains muscle contraction as the sliding of thin (actin) filaments past thick (myosin) filaments, shortening the sarcomere without changing filament lengths. Cross-bridge cycle: (1) Cocked state (myosin head with ADP+Pi); (2) Actin binding (Ca²⁺-exposed sites); (3) Power stroke (Pi release, myosin pivots, actin pulled ~10 nm); (4) Detachment (ATP binding); (5) Re-energizing (ATP hydrolysis). Each cycle consumes 1 ATP, produces 2-3 pN force. Calcium regulation: At rest, tropomyosin covers myosin-binding sites; Ca²⁺ binds troponin C → tropomyosin moves → sites exposed. Relaxation: SERCA pumps Ca²⁺ into SR → tropomyosin returns → cross-bridges cease. Length-tension relationship: maximal active force at resting sarcomere length (2.0-2.2 μm) where overlap optimal; shorter/longer lengths reduce force. Force-velocity relationship: lighter loads → faster shortening; heavier loads → slower; eccentric contractions produce greater force. Energy sources: creatine phosphate (immediate, 5-10 sec), glycolysis, oxidative phosphorylation. Myosin ATPase isoforms: Type I (slow, low ATPase), Type IIb (fast, high ATPase) determine contraction speed. Clinical correlations: Rigor mortis (ATP depletion → cross-bridges locked); hypertrophic cardiomyopathy (β-myosin mutations → hypercontractility); nemaline myopathy (thin filament mutations → nemaline rods); malignant hyperthermia (RyR1 mutations → uncontrolled Ca²⁺ release); myasthenia gravis (ACh receptor antibodies → reduced activation). The sliding filament theory provides the molecular foundation for understanding muscle force generation, regulation, and dysfunction, integrating structure, biochemistry, and physiology into a unified framework of contraction.
